episode_title: How to make better decisions and build a joyful career | Ada Chen Rekhi (Notejoy, LinkedIn, SurveyMonkey)
insight: When facing a big decision, don't just ask for vague advice; run a structured "Curiosity Loop" to get specific, contextual input from a curated group.
pull_quote: A curiosity loop is essentially going to a bunch of people asking, 'Here are 9 topics, what are 2-3 that resonate with you and why?' I got back an incredible amount of information for about 20 minutes of work.

episode_title: How to make better decisions and build a joyful career | Ada Chen Rekhi (Notejoy, LinkedIn, SurveyMonkey)
insight: Don't let the "outer scorecard" of status and titles dictate your career; define your personal values to guide decisions toward genuine fulfillment.
pull_quote: It's this internal scorecard of what really matters to you in your decision-making process, as opposed to the external scorecard of status, money, wealth, and how other people perceive you.

episode_title: How to make better decisions and build a joyful career | Ada Chen Rekhi (Notejoy, LinkedIn, SurveyMonkey)
insight: Early in your career, prioritize "exploration" with a thesis to discover what truly fits, then "exploit" those findings to gain depth and expertise.
pull_quote: When I think about explore and exploit for early career, it's all about exploration. You really haven't experienced that much, but you're doing it with a thesis... My big thesis was, let me try being a founder.

episode_title: How to make better decisions and build a joyful career | Ada Chen Rekhi (Notejoy, LinkedIn, SurveyMonkey)
insight: Most people don't need a coach; often, a structured curiosity loop, a targeted course, or building a strong community offers better, more contextual support.
pull_quote: My hot take is that for the vast majority of people, they probably do not need a coach... If you're looking for a mentor, a coach is actually a terrible mentor because it's just this one person's opinion.

episode_title: How to make better decisions and build a joyful career | Ada Chen Rekhi (Notejoy, LinkedIn, SurveyMonkey)
insight: Many "rules of the game" for career success are unspoken, so you must actively seek out tough feedback to learn how to play at a high level.
pull_quote: The difficulty of this game is that no one tells you the rules of the game... It's so unsafe for your manager to turn to a young woman... and say, 'Here, let me give you feedback on your physical appearance...'

episode_title: How to build a high-performing growth team | Adam Fishman (Patreon, Lyft, Imperfect Foods)
insight: Most PMs obsess over new features when the real unlock is just fixing onboarding — that alone moved retention 10-20 points.
pull_quote: Fixing onboarding is a massive growth lever. I've seen companies shift their cohort retention curves outward by 10-20 percentage points just by making those changes, especially since churn often happens earliest.

episode_title: How to build a high-performing growth team | Adam Fishman (Patreon, Lyft, Imperfect Foods)
insight: When hiring your first growth person, prioritize candidates with strong "growth execution" and "customer knowledge" skills; "strategy" and "influence" come later.
pull_quote: You're not looking for a unicorn. For an early growth hire, focus on execution and customer knowledge. Otherwise, that driven person will just run through brick walls, missing the open door next to them.

episode_title: How to build a high-performing growth team | Adam Fishman (Patreon, Lyft, Imperfect Foods)
insight: Don't obsess over onboarding conversion; sometimes, slightly decreasing it is good if it weeds out bad-fit users, ultimately boosting long-term retention.
pull_quote: If you're doing onboarding right, conversion might actually decrease a little. That's okay – those users weren't the right fit anyway, meaning you're improving retention by pre-qualifying customers.

episode_title: How to build a high-performing growth team | Adam Fishman (Patreon, Lyft, Imperfect Foods)
insight: When evaluating a new company, rigorously back-channel references and ask leaders how they navigate disagreements in C-suite meetings to assess the "people" fit.
pull_quote: Don't just trust interviews. Ask leaders about their last strategic disagreement and how it was resolved. Also, back-channel current or former employees not on the interview circuit for candid feedback on the team dynamics.

episode_title: How to build a high-performing growth team | Adam Fishman (Patreon, Lyft, Imperfect Foods)
insight: After human intervention reveals a growth unlock, productize those learnings into "opinionated defaults" and smart guardrails to scale their impact across your user base.
pull_quote: We saw a 25% revenue increase from human intervention in onboarding. The next step? Productize those learnings into "opinionated defaults" – make it easy to do the right thing, hard to do the wrong one, at scale.

episode_title: When and how to invest in new acquisition channels | Adam Grenier (Uber, MasterClass)
insight: Assume you no longer have product market fit when the market changes; you had it in a *different* market, now you must start over.
pull_quote: Start by assuming you no longer have product market fit, because you had product market fit in a different market. It's a different market now, so you have to start over.

episode_title: When and how to invest in new acquisition channels | Adam Grenier (Uber, MasterClass)
insight: Partner with emerging channels by aligning your business's monetization with their strategy; it gives you a huge leg up and custom opportunities.
pull_quote: If you, as a business, can match or support their monetization strategy, it actually gives you a really interesting leg up with that channel. The likelihood of you doing custom stuff or partnering goes up drastically.

episode_title: When and how to invest in new acquisition channels | Adam Grenier (Uber, MasterClass)
insight: Product is no longer just one of the 4 P's of marketing; it *is* the company, requiring deep marketing integration into every part of it.
pull_quote: The traditional model of marketing is the four P's. In my mind, the product is no longer a part of marketing, but they're married at the hip. The product, it is the company now.

episode_title: When and how to invest in new acquisition channels | Adam Grenier (Uber, MasterClass)
insight: What feels like burnout can actually be depression; distinguish them by observing if broader motivations beyond work are also impacted.
pull_quote: For me, I personally now can pretty cleanly tell the difference between exhaustion and depression. It tends to tie to my broader motivations, not just my motivation to work.

episode_title: Humanizing product development | Adriel Frederick (Reddit, Lyft, Facebook)
insight: Don't let algorithms run wild; PMs must define what machines do, where humans intervene, and the decision-making framework.
pull_quote: Techno-utopians say feed all data to the algorithm, give it an objective, and it will do the right thing. But algorithms don't understand long-term effects, human response, or your intent for the product. PMs must figure out what algorithms are responsible for, what people are responsible for, and the decision framework.

episode_title: Humanizing product development | Adriel Frederick (Reddit, Lyft, Facebook)
insight: In times of controversy, buffer your team, find the *truth* behind the criticism by getting close to users, and don't get lost in the noise.
pull_quote: During controversy, PMs must buffer the team. Don't get lost in the press; find the *truth* behind criticism. I drove for Lyft myself to understand driver complaints about pickup pay, revealing issues the algorithm missed. Stay focused on solving for customers.

episode_title: Humanizing product development | Adriel Frederick (Reddit, Lyft, Facebook)
insight: For complex systems with real-world impact, operational control isn't an afterthought; it's a first-order product design requirement.
pull_quote: We struggled for months to change prices with a new algorithm. The big learning? For products with real-world operations, operational control and putting people in the loop is a *first-order requirement*, not an afterthought caught up in algorithmic complexity.

episode_title: Humanizing product development | Adriel Frederick (Reddit, Lyft, Facebook)
insight: Growth hacking is useful for initial traction, but without fundamental product value, you're just a one-hit wonder; users catch on.
pull_quote: Growth hacking can feel disrespectful if you don't have real fundamental value. People catch on quickly. The products that really moved the needle for us just focused on making the core actions stupid easy for the marginal user. That's the real grind.

episode_title: Humanizing product development | Adriel Frederick (Reddit, Lyft, Facebook)
insight: The power of a simple, concrete activation milestone isn't its perfect correlation, but its ability to galvanize an organization.
pull_quote: Facebook's '10 friends in 14 days' wasn't magical because of the exact number; it was brilliant because it created a concrete goal everyone could chase. That organizational momentum mattered far more than perfect metric correlation – even if the graph dropped at 11 or 12 friends.

episode_title: Aishwarya Naresh Reganti + Kiriti Badam
insight: AI products fundamentally differ from traditional software in their non-determinism and the constant agency-control trade-off.
pull_quote: Most people ignore AI's non-determinism – you don't know how users or the LLM will behave. And with agentic systems, giving more agency means relinquishing control, a crucial trade-off often overlooked.

episode_title: Aishwarya Naresh Reganti + Kiriti Badam
insight: Don't jump to fully autonomous agents; start with high human control and low agency to understand capabilities and avoid complexity.
pull_quote: You don't start with fully autonomous agents on day one. Begin with minimal impact, high human control, then slowly increase agency. This forces you to stay problem-first, instead of getting lost in solution complexity.

episode_title: Aishwarya Naresh Reganti + Kiriti Badam
insight: Leaders must relearn their intuitions for AI, getting hands-on and embracing vulnerability to guide effective company-wide adoption.
pull_quote: Leaders’ intuitions, built over decades, must be relearned with AI. You have to be vulnerable, get hands-on, and learn from everyone, comfortable being the 'dumbest person in the room' to truly drive AI transformation.

episode_title: Aishwarya Naresh Reganti + Kiriti Badam
insight: Successful AI product teams obsess over understanding complex workflows, not just fancy tech, to pick the right tools for real problems.
pull_quote: The most successful AI teams are obsessed with deeply understanding their workflows, not just the technology. You need to deconstruct processes to know where AI truly fits, not just slap an agent on everything and expect magic.

episode_title: Aishwarya Naresh Reganti + Kiriti Badam
insight: In AI, persistence through the pain of learning and iterating on what works and doesn't is the new moat for individuals and companies.
pull_quote: Pain is the new moat. Successful AI companies aren't just first-to-market; they've gone through the pain of learning, implementing, and understanding what truly works. That hard-won knowledge translates into a real competitive advantage.

episode_title: How to find hidden growth opportunities in your product | Albert Cheng (Duolingo, Grammarly, Chess.com)
insight: Don't fear giving free users a taste of premium features; it shows them the product's full power and can nearly double conversion.
pull_quote: We sampled paid features to free users, fearing it would hurt upgrades. Instead, people saw Grammarly as much more powerful, and upgrade rates nearly doubled. It's a reverse free trial, in real time.

episode_title: How to find hidden growth opportunities in your product | Albert Cheng (Duolingo, Grammarly, Chess.com)
insight: Unexpected user behavior, like reviewing wins more than losses, is gold—align your product's approach to human psychology for massive engagement.
pull_quote: We thought people would review losses to fix mistakes. But 80% review wins. We flipped the product to celebrate wins after losses, boosting reviews 25%, subscriptions 20%, and retention a lot.

episode_title: How to find hidden growth opportunities in your product | Albert Cheng (Duolingo, Grammarly, Chess.com)
insight: In a rapidly changing world, prioritize high agency, clock speed, and a beginner's mind over deep experience, which can be a crutch.
pull_quote: I saw the highest performers had high agency and clock speed, not necessarily deep experience. Sometimes experience is a crutch, especially with AI; learned habits need to be discarded.

episode_title: How to find hidden growth opportunities in your product | Albert Cheng (Duolingo, Grammarly, Chess.com)
insight: Don't think of brand marketing and growth experimentation as separate; they combine as rocket fuel to drive massive user acquisition.
pull_quote: I used to think brand and growth were separate, but at Duolingo, the owl's personality in push notifications, then leveraged on TikTok, brought in 20-30% of new users daily.

episode_title: How to find hidden growth opportunities in your product | Albert Cheng (Duolingo, Grammarly, Chess.com)
insight: For mature products, investing in a tailored 'resurrection' experience for dormant users is a huge, often overlooked, growth lever.
pull_quote: For mature companies, a big part of your active user base is reactivated users. Invest in excellent 'resurrected' experiences and novel ways to bring them back; it yields big ROI.

episode_title: Alex Hardimen
insight: At a mission-driven company, business goals serve the mission, defining impact beyond just metrics to include societal change.
pull_quote: Our impact and our business goals are in service of our mission, which is to seek the truth and help people understand the world, not the other way around. That means impact for us is growing subscribers, but it's also when a deeply reported story triggers an important policy change or a new law.

episode_title: Alex Hardimen
insight: Embedding product-minded editors directly into consumer product teams marries journalistic expertise with product signal for superior solutions.
pull_quote: The big difference is we also have editors, if it's a product space that directly shapes our journalism. It brings in the best of their expertise and marries it with a lot of the normal signal that you look for in terms of data research and other insights.

episode_title: Alex Hardimen
insight: In times of public crisis, product teams must blow up roadmaps and pivot entirely to meet urgent societal needs, not just business goals.
pull_quote: As a product leader, it's a real wartime moment where you just need to blow up roadmaps and say, 'Okay, everyone, we have a totally different mandate.' We built a comprehensive public data set of COVID cases when nobody else was doing it.

episode_title: Alex Hardimen
insight: When acquiring beloved products, prioritize not interfering with core magic, even if it means complex backend work and transparent communication.
pull_quote: I'm really proud of how thoughtful and considerate our games team was about the Wordle integration because players feel such connection to the game; we didn't want to interfere with the core magic. We even had to come clean when a horrible word like 'fetus' was scheduled.

episode_title: Alex Hardimen
insight: Unlike platforms, news product algorithms are trained on editorial judgment scores from journalists, balancing expertise with engagement.
pull_quote: We're training algorithms on specific data sets, like editorial important scores that actually come from our journalists. That allows us to scale editorial judgment to a large group of readers, not just optimize for engagement.

episode_title: Thinking like a gardener not a builder, organizing teams like slime mold, the adjacent possible, and other unconventional product advice | Alex Komoroske (Stripe, Google)
insight: Stop just building to a rigid plan; instead, garden for miracles by planting seeds that can grow on their own and yield outsized returns.
pull_quote: So much of how we build products is this builder mindset: I have a plan, I manipulate things to match. That can't create more value than the effort you put in. I look for things that can be gardened, that grow on their own. If you do this properly, it looks like magic.

episode_title: Thinking like a gardener not a builder, organizing teams like slime mold, the adjacent possible, and other unconventional product advice | Alex Komoroske (Stripe, Google)
insight: LLMs are 'magical duct tape' that fundamentally change software's cost structure, enabling cheap 'good enough' software but making expensive, scaled products harder.
pull_quote: LLMs are truly a disruptive technology. They're formed by society's distilled intuition, operating between human and plain computing cost structures. Software used to be expensive to write and cheap to run; LLMs undermine both of these.

episode_title: Thinking like a gardener not a builder, organizing teams like slime mold, the adjacent possible, and other unconventional product advice | Alex Komoroske (Stripe, Google)
insight: 'Kayfabe' in organizations – everyone pretending a known falsity is true – compounds up the hierarchy, leading to poor decisions and a 'zombie' state.
pull_quote: Kayfabe is a thing that everybody knows is fake and yet everybody acts like is real. It compounds up multiple layers, becoming many orders of magnitude off ground truth, leading to very bad decisions. If you acknowledge it's false, you're in danger of getting knocked out of the game.

episode_title: Thinking like a gardener not a builder, organizing teams like slime mold, the adjacent possible, and other unconventional product advice | Alex Komoroske (Stripe, Google)
insight: Create 'nerd clubs' with strict 'yes, and' norms and diverse participants to foster emergent, bottom-up insights by building on interesting ideas.
pull_quote: I created a secret group called Navel Gazers. This is a collaborative debate environment, only 'yes, and'. If lots of different people in the group are building on the same idea, that's a good sign there is something very interesting going on.

episode_title: Thinking like a gardener not a builder, organizing teams like slime mold, the adjacent possible, and other unconventional product advice | Alex Komoroske (Stripe, Google)
insight: To build big things, combine small, safe, 'adjacent possible' steps with a clear, low-resolution 3-5 year North Star, avoiding random walking or castles in the sky.
pull_quote: The adjacent possible is within arm's reach. You don't need to make the decision to jump to the end. Slice up your decisions into smaller steps that make sense, and combine that with a low-resolution, 3-5 year North Star.

episode_title: The power user’s guide to Codex: parallelizing workflows, planning techniques, advanced context engineering tips, automating code reviews, and more | Alexander Embiricos
insight: In AI, you must launch quickly and learn empirically, because you often don't know what capabilities will work or how users will react.
pull_quote: Because we don't exactly know what capabilities will even come up soon, it's much more important to be very humble and learn empirically, just try things quickly. OpenAI is truly bottoms up.

episode_title: The power user’s guide to Codex: parallelizing workflows, planning techniques, advanced context engineering tips, automating code reviews, and more | Alexander Embiricos
insight: Don't launch AI tools too far into the future; integrate deeply into existing workflows (like IDEs) to unlock explosive growth.
pull_quote: Our first Codex version was too far into the future. The key unlock for 20x growth was integrating with IDEs and CLIs, working directly on users' computers. Dogfooding can be misleading when your internal users live in the future.

episode_title: The power user’s guide to Codex: parallelizing workflows, planning techniques, advanced context engineering tips, automating code reviews, and more | Alexander Embiricos
insight: To build truly effective AI agents that can *do things* with computers, focusing on coding as the core capability is paramount.
pull_quote: For models to do stuff, they're much more effective when they can use a computer. It turns out the best way for models to use computers is simply to write code. So if you want to build any agent, maybe you should be building a coding agent.

episode_title: The power user’s guide to Codex: parallelizing workflows, planning techniques, advanced context engineering tips, automating code reviews, and more | Alexander Embiricos
insight: As AI increasingly writes code, the bottleneck shifts to the less enjoyable task of human code review and validation.
pull_quote: AI now writes a ton of code, but writing code is the fun part. The real bottleneck is validating and reviewing AI-written code, which is often less fun. We're actively building features to help build confidence in AI code.

episode_title: The power user’s guide to Codex: parallelizing workflows, planning techniques, advanced context engineering tips, automating code reviews, and more | Alexander Embiricos
insight: As AI trivializes product building, a deep, meaningful understanding of a specific customer's problems becomes the single most crucial differentiator.
pull_quote: We were in a temporary phase where building products was hard, so product building skill mattered most. Now, if I could choose one thing, it'd be a meaningful understanding of a specific customer's problems. That's what matters most.

episode_title: Scripts for difficult conversations: Giving hard feedback, navigating defensiveness, the three questions you should end every meeting with, more | Alisa Cohn (executive coach)
insight: Avoiding hard feedback doesn't protect your employees; it actively hinders their growth and long-term career success.
pull_quote: My client's employee cried, but the next day she said, 'Thank you, I wish someone had told me that 15 years ago.' Your discomfort is nothing compared to the growth you enable.

episode_title: Scripts for difficult conversations: Giving hard feedback, navigating defensiveness, the three questions you should end every meeting with, more | Alisa Cohn (executive coach)
insight: Leaders mistakenly focus on making employees happy; true engagement and company success actually stem from creating a winning, results-driven culture.
pull_quote: Many leaders try to be the one everyone loves, making people happy. But this 'leads to the demise of your company.' Focus on creating a winning culture, not just happiness.

episode_title: Scripts for difficult conversations: Giving hard feedback, navigating defensiveness, the three questions you should end every meeting with, more | Alisa Cohn (executive coach)
insight: To avoid endless re-meetings, always end with three questions: What did we decide? Who does what by when? Who else needs to know?
pull_quote: Ever wonder why meetings don't stick? You'd get six different answers if you asked 'What did we decide here?' End every meeting with these three questions to ensure actual progress.

episode_title: Scripts for difficult conversations: Giving hard feedback, navigating defensiveness, the three questions you should end every meeting with, more | Alisa Cohn (executive coach)
insight: Founders often skip 'prenup' talks, but proactively aligning on values and vision is critical; misalignment causes 65% of startup failures.
pull_quote: '65% of startups fail because of conflict with founders.' Don't assume alignment. Discuss core values and what 'success' truly means for the company's vision *before* you're too deep to change course.

episode_title: Scripts for difficult conversations: Giving hard feedback, navigating defensiveness, the three questions you should end every meeting with, more | Alisa Cohn (executive coach)
insight: Don't assume; share your 'Personal Operating Manual' detailing communication, pet peeves, and 'gold star' actions to build harmony and reduce conflict.
pull_quote: 'Nobody knows what your operating style is until you tell them.' Share your 'Personal Operating Manual' – communication style, pet peeves, how to earn a gold star – to avoid petty conflicts and boost team harmony.

episode_title: Making an impact through authenticity and curiosity | Ami Vora (CPO at Faire, ex-WhatsApp, FB, IG)
insight: Stop trying to be right; instead, embrace disagreements with genuine curiosity as a chance to learn and drive better outcomes.
pull_quote: I love to be right, but in the working world, that didn't serve me so great. Sublimate your ego and say it's more important to get to the outcome than to be right; otherwise, you cut yourself off from learning from others.

episode_title: Making an impact through authenticity and curiosity | Ami Vora (CPO at Faire, ex-WhatsApp, FB, IG)
insight: When presenting to execs, bring a clear, opinionated recommendation because their "dinosaur brain" can only process a few facts.
pull_quote: Executives have a little tiny dinosaur brain. We can really only hold three facts at the same time. The best service you can do is make a recommendation, because then you are forced to be opinionated and don't get lost in information.

episode_title: Making an impact through authenticity and curiosity | Ami Vora (CPO at Faire, ex-WhatsApp, FB, IG)
insight: To align teams without micromanaging, create a compelling product story or metaphor based on the *feeling* you want users to experience.
pull_quote: If we all agree that the feeling of something should be 'I'm sitting in Dolores Park with my friends on a sunny Saturday,' people will naturally build something more consistent. You buy into the same story, and individual decisions align.

episode_title: Making an impact through authenticity and curiosity | Ami Vora (CPO at Faire, ex-WhatsApp, FB, IG)
insight: Prioritize excellent execution with a "good enough" strategy; customers care about the product in their hands, not abstract plans, and execution drives learning.
pull_quote: Perfect strategy with poor execution means you don't win and learn nothing. A 'pretty good' strategy with perfect execution means you *do* win eventually because you build an execution machine to learn and iterate until your strategy is perfect.

episode_title: Making an impact through authenticity and curiosity | Ami Vora (CPO at Faire, ex-WhatsApp, FB, IG)
insight: As a senior leader, accept that you will primarily make "least bad" suboptimal decisions, as all the easy problems are already solved by others.
pull_quote: As you get more senior, you can only make bad decisions. The only problems you'll see are ones that are fundamentally unsolvable. All you're doing is choosing which branch of suboptimal you're going to put your name on.

episode_title: Behind the product: Replit | Amjad Masad (co-founder and CEO)
insight: AI now allows anyone, even non-technical PMs, to build and deploy a fully functional V1 product in minutes, bypassing engineering bottlenecks.
pull_quote: What we just witnessed is somebody that didn't have to have any technical skill build a real product that people can use in five minutes that looks good and works. You could keep making it better by talking to this agent.

episode_title: Behind the product: Replit | Amjad Masad (co-founder and CEO)
insight: With AI removing development bottlenecks, the most valuable PM skill isn't execution, but generating a constant stream of high-quality product ideas.
pull_quote: Typically, you're bottlenecked where your ideas are not fitting in because they need to be made quickly. Now, making things is easier; you become limited by how fast you can generate ideas.

episode_title: Behind the product: Replit | Amjad Masad (co-founder and CEO)
insight: Amjad's Law: the ROI for learning basic AI-native coding and debugging doubles every six months, disproportionately empowering PMs.
pull_quote: The return on investment for learning to code is doubling every six months. Learning a bit about how to prompt AI, read code, and debug nets you more and more power.

episode_title: Behind the product: Replit | Amjad Masad (co-founder and CEO)
insight: Building for today's AI is a trap; true innovation comes from anticipating and designing for AI models landing six months in the future.
pull_quote: It's easy to build and optimize for today's AI. We were like, 'we're going to build for the models that are landing in six months.' Truly, six months later, the models started to land that are capable of this.

episode_title: Behind the product: Replit | Amjad Masad (co-founder and CEO)
insight: Rapid AI advancements mean rigid roadmaps are dead; companies must be fluid, ditching plans to jump on new capabilities immediately.
pull_quote: The way we work is going to change rapidly. It's important to be resilient to that change. When Anthropic dropped a capability, we immediately jumped on it and started building. You want to react quickly.

episode_title: I’ve run 75+ businesses. Here’s why you’re probably chasing the wrong idea. | Andrew Wilkinson (co‑founder of Tiny)
insight: Stop chasing flashy ideas everyone else wants; the real money is in boring, low-competition niches where fewer people 'fish.'
pull_quote: Charlie Munger says, 'Fish where the fish are.' You actually want to find a small fishing hole with lots of fish and very little competition. This means boring ideas like form-filling software can be far more profitable than the next hot AI company.

episode_title: I’ve run 75+ businesses. Here’s why you’re probably chasing the wrong idea. | Andrew Wilkinson (co‑founder of Tiny)
insight: When hiring leaders, realize they're 'elephants' who will go their own way; hire only if their vision aligns or is clearly superior to yours.
pull_quote: Hiring CEOs is like hiring an elephant—they're going to go wherever they want. Don't try to pull them in your direction; hire someone whose vision for the business already makes you nod along.

episode_title: I’ve run 75+ businesses. Here’s why you’re probably chasing the wrong idea. | Andrew Wilkinson (co‑founder of Tiny)
insight: AI agents can become your hyper-reliable personal assistant, automating tasks from email triage to meeting prep and calendar management for just $200 a month.
pull_quote: It's like having the world's most reliable employee who costs $200 a month and works 24/7. My AI agent handles email triage, schedules meetings, and preps me for calls, replacing tasks my full-time assistant used to do.

episode_title: I’ve run 75+ businesses. Here’s why you’re probably chasing the wrong idea. | Andrew Wilkinson (co‑founder of Tiny)
insight: Focus on building businesses that are 'hard to mess up' by prioritizing strong moats like network effects or powerful brands over operational complexity.
pull_quote: When buying a business, I look for one so good it’s hard to mess up. Most businesses are easy to ruin, but those with strong moats like network effects (e.g., Letterboxd) or powerful brands will withstand anything.

episode_title: When enough is enough | Andy Johns (ex-FB, Twitter, Quora)
insight: Don't ignore physical and relational breakdowns; your body is screaming 'stop' when sleep, relationships, or health consistently fail, it's not just 'stress.'
pull_quote: When your fundamental functions – diet, exercise, playfulness, socialization, sleep – get disrupted, it's a flashing red alarm. If your sleep always sucks, relationships are strained, or physical health fails, your body is telling you, 'Stop. Something needs to change.'

episode_title: When enough is enough | Andy Johns (ex-FB, Twitter, Quora)
insight: Many high-achieving PMs become addicted to success, unconsciously tying their self-worth to career milestones, which leads to deep internal suffering despite external success.
pull_quote: I had a strong commitment to my career, and frankly an addiction to achievement as a way to feel good and to feel whole. As my career reached its pinnacle, professionally I was at my highest, but in other aspects of my life, I was arguably at my lowest.

episode_title: When enough is enough | Andy Johns (ex-FB, Twitter, Quora)
insight: Deep personal transformation, essential for lasting happiness, begins with sustained suffering and a dedicated, often multi-year, search for the underlying truth of why you suffer.
pull_quote: These large transitions in life rarely take place in the absence of suffering. Once the suffering gets so bad, you decide to change, and to change, you have to understand the truth as to who you are and why you're suffering.

episode_title: When enough is enough | Andy Johns (ex-FB, Twitter, Quora)
insight: PMs often suppress their unique individuality to meet societal and professional expectations, fearing rejection, which prevents true self-expression and fulfillment.
pull_quote: We choose to push down our individuality in exchange for being accepted by the pack – our parents, friends, teachers, bosses. We lose who we were before the world told us who to be, preventing us from transforming.

episode_title: When enough is enough | Andy Johns (ex-FB, Twitter, Quora)
insight: When seeking personal or career growth, you must find your *own* unique 'road to Bangkok,' as others' successful paths may not work for you.
pull_quote: Everyone's trying to make it to Bangkok. The problem is they're getting to Bangkok by following somebody else's road. The whole point is to find your own path to Bangkok.

episode_title: The power of strategic narrative | Andy Raskin
insight: Stop pitching your solution as merely better; instead, frame your product as the essential way to win in a fundamentally new world.
pull_quote: Most pitches are the 'arrogant doctor': problem, solution, why mine's better. But truly impactful pitches define a movement, showing a shift from an old game to a new one, and how you help people win.

episode_title: The power of strategic narrative | Andy Raskin
insight: A compelling strategic narrative serves as a crucial filter for product roadmap decisions, cutting through endless feature requests.
pull_quote: CEOs tell me the strategic narrative becomes 'the strategic north star for the product.' It's the bar to decide what feature requests we take on and what we don't.

episode_title: The power of strategic narrative | Andy Raskin
insight: Don't obsess over a 'category name' — the powerful 'movement' story behind it is what truly differentiates you, not the label itself.
pull_quote: With Gong, the CEO said, 'We probably could have called it strawberry intelligence.' It didn't matter, it was the story behind it that mattered. Focus on movement creation, not just category names.

episode_title: The power of strategic narrative | Andy Raskin
insight: Expect the second draft of your strategic narrative to be 'shit' and frustrating; it's a necessary step to get to a truly powerful story.
pull_quote: The CEO and I will present our first draft, and it's basically throwing out all the team's initial ideas. It's painful, but a 'shit draft' is a million times more valuable than just ideas.

episode_title: Becoming more strategic, navigating difficult colleagues, harnessing founder mode, and more | Anneka Gupta (Chief Product Officer at Rubrik)
insight: True strategic thinking isn't just about big ideas; it's about clearly articulating the 'why' and being a change agent for hard, long-term decisions.
pull_quote: When people say 'I want someone that's strategic,' they really mean 'I want someone that can come up with and articulate a compelling and simple why' and 'champion and be a change agent to do things that may be hard but best for the long-term interest.'

episode_title: Becoming more strategic, navigating difficult colleagues, harnessing founder mode, and more | Anneka Gupta (Chief Product Officer at Rubrik)
insight: View your founder's power as a strategic lever to push through initiatives you know are best for the company, rather than just an obstacle.
pull_quote: Activating that founder, that CEO, to really be able to push the initiative that I think is best by making them an ally. Use their power to get things done you know are best for the company.

episode_title: Becoming more strategic, navigating difficult colleagues, harnessing founder mode, and more | Anneka Gupta (Chief Product Officer at Rubrik)
insight: Simply summarizing diverse viewpoints and synthesizing them into a clear direction is often perceived as strategic and helps resolve conflict.
pull_quote: People actually view effective summarization as strategy. It helps bring diverse perspectives together, move beyond conflict, and get to the very heart of an issue—which is what the PM job is all about.

episode_title: Becoming more strategic, navigating difficult colleagues, harnessing founder mode, and more | Anneka Gupta (Chief Product Officer at Rubrik)
insight: Don't let analysis paralysis stop you; make a 70% right decision and commit, because only then will you gain high-fidelity information to iterate and learn.
pull_quote: It's not about making the right decision, it's about making the decision. Once you commit, you learn more about what works. If you don't commit, you don't get new information.

episode_title: Becoming more strategic, navigating difficult colleagues, harnessing founder mode, and more | Anneka Gupta (Chief Product Officer at Rubrik)
insight: Aspiring PMs often focus on learning tools like Figma, but the real core skill is consistently clarifying ambiguity in complex situations.
pull_quote: New PMs often think they need to learn tools like Figma, but the real skill is 'how to take very ambiguous situations and consistently drive more and more clarity over time.' That's the mindset needed for success.

episode_title: This will make you a better decision-maker | Annie Duke (author of “Thinking in Bets” and “Quit,” former pro poker player)
insight: Stop brainstorming or deciding in group meetings; the only thing for a meeting is discussing independent inputs.
pull_quote: People generally think meetings are for discovery, discussion, and decision. But the only thing that's ever supposed to happen in a meeting is the discussion part. Get opinions independently beforehand, then come together to discuss the differences.

episode_title: This will make you a better decision-maker | Annie Duke (author of “Thinking in Bets” and “Quit,” former pro poker player)
insight: Stop blaming "long feedback loops"—you always choose how long your feedback loop is by tracking correlated signals.
pull_quote: There is no such thing as a long feedback loop. You make a decision about how long it is. Shorten it by asking: what are the things correlated with the outcome I eventually desire? You can track those way earlier.

episode_title: This will make you a better decision-maker | Annie Duke (author of “Thinking in Bets” and “Quit,” former pro poker player)
insight: A pre-mortem is useless unless you set clear "kill criteria" and pre-commit to specific actions if those signals appear.
pull_quote: Pre-mortems are great only if you set up kill criteria. You need a set of signals that tell you when to pivot or stop, and you must commit to actions you'll take when you see them. Otherwise, they don't change behavior.

episode_title: This will make you a better decision-maker | Annie Duke (author of “Thinking in Bets” and “Quit,” former pro poker player)
insight: Make all implicit judgments explicit—only then can you analyze, learn, and improve your intuition when it's wrong.
pull_quote: Our intuition is sometimes right, sometimes wrong. The only way to find out when it's wrong and truly improve decision-making is to take what's implicit—all your assumptions and judgments—and make them explicit.

episode_title: This will make you a better decision-maker | Annie Duke (author of “Thinking in Bets” and “Quit,” former pro poker player)
insight: The true cost of not quitting a failing project isn't just wasted resources, it's the invisible opportunities you miss.
pull_quote: When you're doing something, there's the direct cost, but also the cost of not devoting attention to other opportunities. Stewart Butterfield couldn't see Slack until he quit Glitch. If he'd continued, Slack wouldn't exist.

episode_title: Behind the scenes of Calendly’s rapid growth | Annie Pearl (CPO)
insight: Calendly's initial viral loop started accidentally when their dev firm's other clients became their first users, then spread it via a compelling free product.
pull_quote: The first 10 users were actually customer success agents at a company that contracted with the same firm that Tope was using to build Calendly. Then those parents started using Calendly for their own parent-teacher conference scheduling.

episode_title: Behind the scenes of Calendly’s rapid growth | Annie Pearl (CPO)
insight: A clear product strategy lets you confidently say "no" to highly requested features, like a Venmo integration, if they don't serve your primary target personas.
pull_quote: There's lots of small businesses and solopreneurs who would love us to have a Venmo integration. But our target market are sales, recruiting, customer success teams. So, it doesn't make sense within those personas.

episode_title: Behind the scenes of Calendly’s rapid growth | Annie Pearl (CPO)
insight: Instead of committing to distant project delivery dates, only commit to dates for individual phases like discovery or solutioning, allowing for better estimation and flexibility.
pull_quote: We've moved to a model of committing to dates that are within our control. We can commit to a discovery effort, but we don't know if we're going to actually end up going forward with investing in that area.

episode_title: Behind the scenes of Calendly’s rapid growth | Annie Pearl (CPO)
insight: When adding sales to a PLG product, your first hires should be inbound-focused "growers" who understand departmental buyers, not outbound "hunters" used to selling to CIOs.
pull_quote: In those early days, it's more inbound in nature, and so the type of sales reps you might need are not necessarily going to be outbound, heavy kind of hunting sales reps.

episode_title: Behind the scenes of Calendly’s rapid growth | Annie Pearl (CPO)
insight: True strategic prioritization means explicitly allocating resources across defined horizons, like shifting from 70/30 (H1/H2) to 30/60/10 (H1/H2/H3) over years to evolve the product.
pull_quote: In year one, resources for H1/H2 was about a 70/30 split. Year two, it shifted to 50/50. Entering year three, it's 30% in H1, 60% in H2, and 10% in H3.

episode_title: Building Lovable: $10M ARR in 60 days with 15 people | Anton Osika (co-founder and CEO)
insight: Forget "minimum viable product"—the secret to insane growth is building a truly minimum lovable product from day one.
pull_quote: The best word for a great product is that it's lovable. I use the jargon 'minimum lovable product' then 'lovable product' then 'absolutely lovable product.' I took that jargon into the company name.

episode_title: Building Lovable: $10M ARR in 60 days with 15 people | Anton Osika (co-founder and CEO)
insight: Communicating clearly with an AI engineer is *even more crucial* than with a human, requiring extreme specificity to avoid missteps.
pull_quote: Explaining exactly what you expect and what you're not getting is even more important with AI than with humans. Don't just say 'it doesn't work,' explain which parts are working and which are not.

episode_title: Building Lovable: $10M ARR in 60 days with 15 people | Anton Osika (co-founder and CEO)
insight: The real AI breakthrough isn't just generating code, it's meticulously preventing the system from "getting stuck" on critical tasks like login or payments.
pull_quote: When you have AI building something, it can get stuck. We painstakingly identified places where it got stuck, like adding login, data persistence, and payments, and made sure it doesn't get stuck there.

episode_title: Building Lovable: $10M ARR in 60 days with 15 people | Anton Osika (co-founder and CEO)
insight: To build an ultra-fast, world-class team, explicitly filter for obsessive ambition and deep care, embracing that it will deter many.
pull_quote: The most important thing is that people care a lot, not just 'I'm here for a job.' Everyone should really care about the product, users, and team. That care, or preferably obsession, gets you a very long way.

episode_title: Building Lovable: $10M ARR in 60 days with 15 people | Anton Osika (co-founder and CEO)
insight: Even with cutting-edge AI, the simple act of working in-office and eating lunch together can be a surprising unlock for extreme team velocity.
pull_quote: We work from the office most of the time. You can say 'Hey, I think we're thinking wrong about this,' or 'Shouldn't we actually do this?' Lunch, eating lunch together is a productive hour where you're cross-pollinating.

episode_title: The full-stack PM | Anuj Rathi (Swiggy, Jupiter Money, Flipkart)
insight: Most PMs obsess over core features, but the real product success comes from empathizing with a new user who is lazy, vain, and selfish.
pull_quote: You have to think about users on modern internet consumers having three attributes: they are lazy, they are vain, and they're selfish. The craft of thinking like a user who's lazy, vain, and selfish, and basically, rejecting all your products, is extremely hard, but it's totally worth it.

episode_title: The full-stack PM | Anuj Rathi (Swiggy, Jupiter Money, Flipkart)
insight: Don't just work backwards from one press release; create three fully thought-out, divergent PR FAQs to ensure comprehensive discovery and leadership alignment.
pull_quote: I actually ask my teams to write three press releases, alternative and divergent. When you're doing this three PR FAQs, I considered this and created a story, but unfortunately, it is not adding up, so we rejected it. So, now, people can compare and contrast, and that's a very powerful leadership tool.

episode_title: The full-stack PM | Anuj Rathi (Swiggy, Jupiter Money, Flipkart)
insight: Strategic prioritization means leadership aligning on investment across Brilliant Basics, Bread & Butter, Big Bets, and Breaking Bad, not just PMs.
pull_quote: Prioritizing between these four buckets really is a product strategy call, and the conversation that needs to happen is between the head of product and the CEO. If I gave you a hundred focus points, how much will you put in each of these buckets?

episode_title: The full-stack PM | Anuj Rathi (Swiggy, Jupiter Money, Flipkart)
insight: Traditional OKRs fundamentally fail in multi-sided marketplaces because goals for different user types are inherently conflicting.
pull_quote: OKRs will not work [in marketplaces]. The fundamental assumption is that it is solving for one kind of user... but if it is working for three different kinds of users, then all the goals will all the time be in conflict with each other. It's almost impossible to do that.

episode_title: Microsoft CPO: If you aren’t prototyping with AI, you’re doing it wrong | Aparna Chennapragada
insight: In the AI era, PMs *must* prototype and build immediately to avoid a 'Frankenstein product,' as prompt sets are the new PRDs for communication.
pull_quote: If you're not prototyping and building to see what you want to build, you're doing it wrong. Prompt sets are the new PRDs, offering the fastest path to communicate your product vision.

episode_title: Microsoft CPO: If you aren’t prototyping with AI, you’re doing it wrong | Aparna Chennapragada
insight: Don't just chat with AI; PMs must explicitly *design* natural language interfaces (NLX), as conversations have invisible UI elements and grammars.
pull_quote: Natural language interface (NLX) is the new UX. Conversations have grammars, structures, and invisible UI elements. Product builders, you must explicitly design for this.

episode_title: Microsoft CPO: If you aren’t prototyping with AI, you’re doing it wrong | Aparna Chennapragada
insight: AI will automate process-heavy PM tasks, but it will elevate the strategic importance of PMs as 'editors' and taste-makers to curate the explosion of new ideas.
pull_quote: AI will automate the 'TPS report' PMs out of a job. But the true value of PMs as 'editors' and taste-makers for the explosion of new ideas? That becomes even *more* vital.

episode_title: Microsoft CPO: If you aren’t prototyping with AI, you’re doing it wrong | Aparna Chennapragada
insight: Product builders constantly underestimate AI's current capabilities because it's hard to update our priors; challenge past failures to unlock new potential.
pull_quote: It's hard to update your priors. AI models couldn't do things a year ago that they can do today. Ignore what you *thought* it couldn't do and demand more from the AI.

episode_title: Microsoft CPO: If you aren’t prototyping with AI, you’re doing it wrong | Aparna Chennapragada
insight: For a truly great zero-to-one product, you need at least two out of three inflection points: a tech shift, a consumer behavior shift, or a business model shift.
pull_quote: To nail a zero-to-one product, you need at least two of these: a step-function tech shift, a major consumer behavior shift, or a new business model inflection point.

episode_title: April Dunford on product positioning, segmentation, and optimizing your sales process
insight: Most PMs obsess over direct rivals, but you often lose 40% of deals to 'no decision'—meaning you lost to the spreadsheet.
pull_quote: In B2B, we lose about 40% of our deals to quote-unquote, 'no decision,' which actually means we lost to the spreadsheet, we lost to pen and paper, we lost to interns. If you're not positioning well against that, you're never going to get the customer to come off of it.

episode_title: April Dunford on product positioning, segmentation, and optimizing your sales process
insight: In early stages, keep your product's positioning loose; let the market pull you to discover where the true value lies.
pull_quote: It's better in the early days of a product to keep the positioning a little bit loose and allow the market to pull you maybe in a direction that you didn't think it was going to. Don't over-tighten until you see clear patterns.

episode_title: April Dunford on product positioning, segmentation, and optimizing your sales process
insight: Focus on one B2B persona: the 'champion' who sells your product internally; ignore a dozen useless persona sheets.
pull_quote: The most important persona that matters is the 'champion' in the account. If our positioning doesn't resonate for that champion, we're dead. It's an utter waste of time for marketing to build these stupid little one-pager persona things for 17 million personas.

episode_title: April Dunford on product positioning, segmentation, and optimizing your sales process
insight: When prospects say, 'I get what you do, but why would I pay for that?' it's a critical sign your value proposition is missing.
pull_quote: You'll get this terrible one where people will say, 'Well, I get it. I mean, I totally get it. I just don't get why anyone would pay for that. I can do that in a spreadsheet.' They think they get what you do, but don't understand the value.

episode_title: April Dunford on product positioning, segmentation, and optimizing your sales process
insight: Most weak positioning stems from internal misalignment; the founder, product, sales, and marketing tell different product stories.
pull_quote: A lot of weak positioning comes from the fact that we don't have perfect alignment across the team. The founder has one idea, marketing's a little different, sales is different again. It needs to be a group effort to align everyone.

episode_title: Breaking the rules of growth: Why Shopify bans KPIs, optimizes for churn, prioritizes intuition, and builds toward a 100-year vision | Archie Abrams (VP Product, Head of Growth at Shopify)
insight: That quick win from your experiment? It's probably just a pull-forward effect; 30-40% of short-term lifts show no long-term impact on core business value.
pull_quote: We constantly re-look at experiments a year later. You'd be surprised how often things look different. Roughly 30-40% of short-term lifts show no long-term impact on GMV. Many are just pulling success forward.

episode_title: Breaking the rules of growth: Why Shopify bans KPIs, optimizes for churn, prioritizes intuition, and builds toward a 100-year vision | Archie Abrams (VP Product, Head of Growth at Shopify)
insight: Most companies want to keep every customer, but we actively optimize for churn because our power law business model thrives on many attempts for a few big winners.
pull_quote: Most businesses fail, so we lower barriers to entry. The few successful merchants in a cohort generate so much GMV, they make the entire cohort—and Shopify's business—successful.

episode_title: Breaking the rules of growth: Why Shopify bans KPIs, optimizes for churn, prioritizes intuition, and builds toward a 100-year vision | Archie Abrams (VP Product, Head of Growth at Shopify)
insight: Don't let teams optimize local conversion rates; they'll just make the prior step harder. Instead, measure them on the absolute number of people who get to their stage.
pull_quote: The easiest way to increase retention is always to constrict the funnel stage one above. If teams focus on local conversion rates, they optimize to implicitly make it harder to do the step before them.

episode_title: Breaking the rules of growth: Why Shopify bans KPIs, optimizes for churn, prioritizes intuition, and builds toward a 100-year vision | Archie Abrams (VP Product, Head of Growth at Shopify)
insight: For a technology company building for 100 years, the “technical how” (architecture) determines your future strategy even more than *what* or *who* you’re building for.
pull_quote: Our CEO, Tobi, says the technical architecture determines strategy in a technology company even more than the what and who we're building for. We debate CSV importers because the 'how' is crucial for optionality.

episode_title: The art of building legendary brands | Arielle Jackson (Google, Square, Marketer in Residence at First Round Capital)
insight: Don't stress endlessly over your startup name; a bad name won't kill a good company, and a great product will imbue meaning into any name.
pull_quote: A bad name with a really great company with great company strategy, great marketing is going to be great over time. And a good name is just going to help you, but I don't think a bad name is going to kill a good company.

episode_title: The art of building legendary brands | Arielle Jackson (Google, Square, Marketer in Residence at First Round Capital)
insight: A small upfront investment in brand strategy, completed in weeks, saves significant time down the road by clarifying decisions and even automating copy.
pull_quote: Whatever time it takes you actually is going to save you so much time down the road. It's going to help you save time on company decision making. It's going to help you save time, writing your website. Literally your web copy almost writes itself.

episode_title: The art of building legendary brands | Arielle Jackson (Google, Square, Marketer in Residence at First Round Capital)
insight: If asking 10 customers or employees what your product does yields 10 wildly different answers, you definitely have a positioning problem.
pull_quote: You have a positioning problem if I ask 10 of your customers or 10 of your employees what the company does or what the product does. And I get multiple answers.

episode_title: The art of building legendary brands | Arielle Jackson (Google, Square, Marketer in Residence at First Round Capital)
insight: Use the "bar test": pretend to be your target user explaining your product's category and benefit to a friend, ensuring the language sounds natural and prompts follow-up questions.
pull_quote: Now you pretend to be someone in your target, having drinks with someone else in your target at a bar and you have to be able to say, hey, I just started using product name. It's this really great category, that benefit. And the other person goes, hm, tell me more.

episode_title: The art of building legendary brands | Arielle Jackson (Google, Square, Marketer in Residence at First Round Capital)
insight: Most startups overlook local press, but if you can frame your local customers as heroes, these outlets are often much hungrier for stories than national tech media.
pull_quote: Don't sleep on local press. If you have a local business or a local story or local customers, this was a huge thing at Square. We turned all of our customers into heroes and went after local press.

episode_title: How 80,000 companies build with AI: products as organisms, the death of org charts, and why agents will outnumber employees by 2026 | Asha Sharma (CVP of AI Platform at Microsoft)
insight: Products are no longer static artifacts but living organisms that continuously improve with user interaction and data, becoming a company's new IP.
pull_quote: Products aren't just static artifacts we ship. They're living organisms that get better with every interaction, making them the new IP of every company. It's a completely different way to build products that think, live, and learn.

episode_title: How 80,000 companies build with AI: products as organisms, the death of org charts, and why agents will outnumber employees by 2026 | Asha Sharma (CVP of AI Platform at Microsoft)
insight: As marginal output cost approaches zero, agents will drive exponential productivity, turning traditional 'org charts' into dynamic 'work charts' focused on task throughput.
pull_quote: The marginal cost of good output is approaching zero, leading to exponential demand for productivity. Agents scale that. When that happens, the org chart becomes the work chart—you just don't need as many layers.

episode_title: How 80,000 companies build with AI: products as organisms, the death of org charts, and why agents will outnumber employees by 2026 | Asha Sharma (CVP of AI Platform at Microsoft)
insight: The future of building successful AI products demands full-stack 'polymaths' obsessed with the entire learning loop, not just their functional 'lane'.
pull_quote: The old way of 500 touchpoints to ship a product is insufficient when 500 new technologies emerge weekly. We're seeing the rise of the full-stack builder, obsessed with the 'loop, not the lane' for true velocity.

episode_title: How 80,000 companies build with AI: products as organisms, the death of org charts, and why agents will outnumber employees by 2026 | Asha Sharma (CVP of AI Platform at Microsoft)
insight: Forget building models from scratch; the real leverage and economic sense now comes from heavily investing in post-training like reinforcement learning on existing foundation models.
pull_quote: Once a model hits 30 billion parameters, the CapEx for pre-training doesn't economically make sense. We'll spend as much, if not more, on post-training—fine-tuning and reinforcement learning to optimize for specific outcomes.

episode_title: How 80,000 companies build with AI: products as organisms, the death of org charts, and why agents will outnumber employees by 2026 | Asha Sharma (CVP of AI Platform at Microsoft)
insight: True platform success isn't about flashy features but mastering the invisible work: the fundamental reliability, performance, and privacy that underpin user trust and usage.
pull_quote: WhatsApp didn't win with stickers; it won on reliability, speed, and privacy. Instacart isn't just loved features; it's optimizing a billion items 3,000 times a minute. Success is in the invisible infrastructure, not the pixels.

episode_title: The ultimate guide to Martech | Austin Hay (Reforge, Ramp, Runway)
insight: MarTech is essentially product management focused on the systems and platforms that enable marketing and growth, not just third-party tools.
pull_quote: Marketing technology is like this very amorphous, cross-functional discipline that lives at the crossroads of product and growth. It's a product manager whose specific role and focus is the system or the third-party or first-party platform.

episode_title: The ultimate guide to Martech | Austin Hay (Reforge, Ramp, Runway)
insight: You need a dedicated MarTech person when your company hits 100-150 people; the 'village approach' to managing marketing tools no longer scales efficiently.
pull_quote: At some point you reach a critical mass where it's no longer manageable by one person or even a set of people. Usually around 100 to 150 people is the critical mass where you can't just have a village approach to systems and tools.

episode_title: The ultimate guide to Martech | Austin Hay (Reforge, Ramp, Runway)
insight: The old B2C data stack with CDPs is evolving; now, with cheaper warehousing, a custom CDP with a reverse ETL from your data warehouse is often more efficient.
pull_quote: Around 2020 the cost of ownership of warehousing became much cheaper. Now you can actually build your own CDP—many businesses already have—and use a reverse ETL to take that data from your warehouse and get it out into the world.

episode_title: The ultimate guide to Martech | Austin Hay (Reforge, Ramp, Runway)
insight: From day one, design your data collection to store first-touch, last-touch, and all middle-touch attribution parameters to enable advanced multi-touch attribution later.
pull_quote: The best practice is to design the system for MTA from day one. Most people don't do this work early on, and when they want multi-touch attribution results later, they simply don't have the data to do it.

episode_title: The ultimate guide to Martech | Austin Hay (Reforge, Ramp, Runway)
insight: With deterministic matching gone, marketers now must learn to make decisions with probabilistic data models to extrapolate campaign impact from partial user data.
pull_quote: From 2010 to 2020, we had the golden years of deterministic matching, but you can't do that anymore. Now, we use other information to create a model for 30% of the population and extrapolate that to a hundred.

episode_title: Frameworks for product differentiation, team building, and thinking from first principles | Ayo Omojola (Carbon Health, Cash App)
insight: Being truly differentiated means your product is both better and different in a way that *matters* to users, not just marginally.
pull_quote: Being different is not enough, because it's very easy to build a thing that's different from what exists today. Being better is not enough... it has to be better than what exists today in a way that matters to the end user.

episode_title: Frameworks for product differentiation, team building, and thinking from first principles | Ayo Omojola (Carbon Health, Cash App)
insight: Internal startups often fail because they lack the existential fear of real startups; combat this with a small, senior, and highly trusted team.
pull_quote: The startup within a startup, everything's fake. The reason it's fake is because when you're a startup you actually worry about paying the people who work for you, and when you aren't inside of a company you just don't.

episode_title: Frameworks for product differentiation, team building, and thinking from first principles | Ayo Omojola (Carbon Health, Cash App)
insight: Hiring founders brings high output and cuts through bureaucracy, but be ready for higher attrition and direct challenges to the status quo.
pull_quote: If there's any waste or bullshit in your organization, like they fucking see it right away and call it out. And you really can only keep them for like two, or two and a half years.

episode_title: Frameworks for product differentiation, team building, and thinking from first principles | Ayo Omojola (Carbon Health, Cash App)
insight: Don't trust the first 'expert' answer; you must go deep and understand every detail yourself, or you're just building on assumptions.
pull_quote: You go ask somebody something, and they give you an answer that they believe to be true... and it's just fucking wonk. You just have to keep pushing until you get to an answer, you can't stop until you get to the end.

episode_title: Frameworks for product differentiation, team building, and thinking from first principles | Ayo Omojola (Carbon Health, Cash App)
insight: In health tech, often success isn't about product merit but who you know; strong networks can unlock critical deals and data.
pull_quote: In healthcare... very often the way to make things happen is network-dependent, not necessarily about the merit of the thing itself. There's companies that exist because the founders know the CEO of every major payer.

episode_title: Unorthodox frameworks for growing your product, career, and impact | Bangaly Kaba (YouTube, Instagram, Facebook, Instacart)
insight: To truly ship more impactful products faster, dedicate planned time to "understand work" rather than blindly executing.
pull_quote: "Slow down and speed up." We saw our experiment win rate on Instagram hit 60-70% because we prioritized 'understand work' to de-risk ideas before full execution. Don't just sprint on features; truly grasp the problem first.

episode_title: Unorthodox frameworks for growing your product, career, and impact | Bangaly Kaba (YouTube, Instagram, Facebook, Instacart)
insight: To capture true hypergrowth, you must deeply understand and use your product from the perspective of the "adjacent user" who isn't a power user yet.
pull_quote: A power user has so much history... if you go and create a new Gmail account and look at YouTube today, I guarantee you it's a completely different and significantly worse experience. Your next users are different; go experience the product like them.

episode_title: Unorthodox frameworks for growing your product, career, and impact | Bangaly Kaba (YouTube, Instagram, Facebook, Instacart)
insight: Don't optimize for compensation or title; focus on driving more impact, as those are just outputs of genuine contribution.
pull_quote: I realized that it's not compensation. Compensation is a reflection of the input, the impact that you're having. The more impact you drive, the more scope and opportunities you'll naturally gain.

episode_title: Unorthodox frameworks for growing your product, career, and impact | Bangaly Kaba (YouTube, Instagram, Facebook, Instacart)
insight: Sustainable hypergrowth isn't one big win; it's about building and compounding two, three, or four layered growth loops together.
pull_quote: Everyone credits Stories for Instagram's growth, but partnerships, SEO, and web presence were equally critical, compounding each other. You need multiple engines magnifying top-of-funnel efforts.

episode_title: Unorthodox frameworks for growing your product, career, and impact | Bangaly Kaba (YouTube, Instagram, Facebook, Instacart)
insight: Your manager is the single most important variable impacting your ability to deliver and feel impact within your environment.
pull_quote: The manager is the most important variable in the environment... they don't leave a job, they leave a manager. A great manager can move the chess pieces to fix issues like scope, resources, or team culture.

episode_title: Category creation and brand building | Barbra Gago (Pando, Miro, Greenhouse, Culture Amp)
insight: You're not truly creating a new category until competitors emerge, as their presence actually validates its existence and market need.
pull_quote: Most people think you want to avoid competition when creating a new category. But it's not officially a category until other companies join, validating its existence. Their entry is a good sign.

episode_title: Category creation and brand building | Barbra Gago (Pando, Miro, Greenhouse, Culture Amp)
insight: Don't force a new category if customers continue using existing terms for budgeting and communication, even if your product is differentiated.
pull_quote: We tried to create a 'recruiting optimization platform' category. But no matter what we called it, customers still budgeted and talked about it as 'applicant tracking.' We had to abandon that effort.

episode_title: Category creation and brand building | Barbra Gago (Pando, Miro, Greenhouse, Culture Amp)
insight: To truly establish a new category, you must influence industry analysts and directory sites, as they shape how customers define and budget for product types.
pull_quote: When creating a new category, don't just rely on content. You *must* engage industry analysts and directory sites like G2 Crowd. They define categories for buyers, so you need their validation.

episode_title: Category creation and brand building | Barbra Gago (Pando, Miro, Greenhouse, Culture Amp)
insight: A rebrand isn't just a marketing task; you must actively involve and align the entire company, treating it as a product development process.
pull_quote: Rebranding is not just a marketing thing; it's a company-wide product approach. You must involve everyone, making it an agile process with sprint teams, because everyone has thoughts and feelings about the brand.

episode_title: Category creation and brand building | Barbra Gago (Pando, Miro, Greenhouse, Culture Amp)
insight: Opinionated software either enables existing best practices with technology or teaches users a demonstrably better, more principled way to work.
pull_quote: Opinionated software works in two ways: either it enables a well-established, tech-lacking best practice (like Atlassian for Agile), or it boldly teaches users a *new, better* way to solve their problems (like Greenhouse for structured recruiting).

episode_title: $46B of hard truths from Ben Horowitz: Why founders fail and why you need to run toward fear (a16z co-founder)
insight: Great leaders run toward horrible choices, picking the slightly less terrible path, even if it brings public ridicule, to avoid worse outcomes.
pull_quote: The worst thing you do as a leader is hesitate when both decisions are horrible. We took our company public with $2M revenue at 18 months, an obvious bad idea. But the alternative was going bankrupt, which is a worse idea.

episode_title: $46B of hard truths from Ben Horowitz: Why founders fail and why you need to run toward fear (a16z co-founder)
insight: Your job as a PM is pure leadership, rallying people to build a product customers love, despite having zero formal authority over them.
pull_quote: The job is fundamentally a leadership job. It's tricky because nobody reports to you. Your actual job is to get a product into market that customers love that's better than anything else in the world.

episode_title: $46B of hard truths from Ben Horowitz: Why founders fail and why you need to run toward fear (a16z co-founder)
insight: As a CEO, don't try to coach people to be world-class in roles you don't master; hire experts to gain leverage, or you'll lose focus.
pull_quote: If you're a CEO, what do you know about being CFO? To think you'll make somebody world-class who isn't, when you don't know anything about it, is a dumb idea. The company can't afford that.

episode_title: $46B of hard truths from Ben Horowitz: Why founders fail and why you need to run toward fear (a16z co-founder)
insight: Building a 'thin wrapper' around foundational AI models is a mistake; true moats are built by creating proprietary models and data at the application layer.
pull_quote: The thing people got very wrong is this whole thin wrapper around GPT. That's really wrong. Look at Cursor: they built 14 different models to truly understand how a developer works. That's real, not a thin layer.

episode_title: How Snyk built a product-led growth juggernaut | Ben Williams (VP of Product at Snyk)
insight: Don't just build features; embed growth directly into the product, like Snyk's auto-fix GitHub PRs that acquire new users.
pull_quote: Snyk created a magic loop: connect GitHub, find vulnerabilities, and it automatically creates Snyk-branded pull requests to *fix* them. Other devs see it, click, and join the loop. It was a primary growth engine.

episode_title: How Snyk built a product-led growth juggernaut | Ben Williams (VP of Product at Snyk)
insight: High user adoption and retention won't lead to enterprise self-serve monetization if you don't meet the broader governance needs of a large buyer.
pull_quote: Snyk had a valuable product, strong user growth, and retention, but early self-serve monetization failed with enterprises. We learned you *must* build table stakes governance features for big buyers, otherwise sales will be critical.

episode_title: How Snyk built a product-led growth juggernaut | Ben Williams (VP of Product at Snyk)
insight: Embed growth marketers directly into cross-functional product teams to solve misaligned incentives and accelerate experiment execution.
pull_quote: We had R&D and growth marketing, with amazing ideas, but misaligned incentives caused friction. We solved it by embedding growth marketers into cross-functional teams, aligning everyone on shared objectives and KPIs. It's not common, but powerful.

episode_title: How Snyk built a product-led growth juggernaut | Ben Williams (VP of Product at Snyk)
insight: Real activation isn't just finding value; it's establishing a habit around *deriving core value*, like fixing issues, often at a team level.
pull_quote: For Snyk, activation isn't just finding vulnerabilities; it's *fixing* them. We define a team as activated if they fix vulnerabilities within 30 days of creation, as it strongly correlates with long-term retention.

episode_title: How Snyk built a product-led growth juggernaut | Ben Williams (VP of Product at Snyk)
insight: Avoid one-size-fits-all fixed trial lengths; tailor trial duration based on company size, complexity, or even usage for better conversion.
pull_quote: Fixed trial lengths are often ineffective because companies of different sizes and industries need different evaluation times. Consider dynamic trial lengths based on usage or other dimensions to better cater to diverse customers.

episode_title: How marketplaces win: Liquidity, growth levers, quality, and more | Benjamin Lauzier (Lyft, Thumbtack, Reforge)
insight: Pre-product market fit, stop obsessing over marketplace dynamics; focus 100% on nailing growth and value for just one side, even if you hack the other.
pull_quote: If you don't have product market fit, and if you don't have a good enough growth strategy for at least one side of your marketplace, just forget about all this marketplace stuff. Focus on this core exchange of value, go deep with one side.

episode_title: How marketplaces win: Liquidity, growth levers, quality, and more | Benjamin Lauzier (Lyft, Thumbtack, Reforge)
insight: While liquidity (fill rate) is the ultimate output, focus teams on an *actionable predictor* like ETA, which directly correlates to conversions and retention.
pull_quote: Your liquidity might be how many of those searches with intent actually turn into a transaction... but the metric that is slightly more actionable... is what I call a market health metric, your proxy that is the best predictor of your liquidity.

episode_title: How marketplaces win: Liquidity, growth levers, quality, and more | Benjamin Lauzier (Lyft, Thumbtack, Reforge)
insight: Giving marketplace users more choice through filters can unexpectedly fragment your supply and drastically hurt service levels, even if they ask for it.
pull_quote: People who build marketplaces tend to want to give a lot of control to the users... but you unknowingly fragment your supply in a way that has a much more meaningful impact on the health of your marketplace than you suspect.

episode_title: How marketplaces win: Liquidity, growth levers, quality, and more | Benjamin Lauzier (Lyft, Thumbtack, Reforge)
insight: Over-managing your supply or trying to control the entire transaction can backfire, eroding intrinsic motivations and risking legal reclassification of your 'partners.'
pull_quote: Any attempt at control can be really tricky and backfire in ways that are unpredictable... my take is I'm a huge believer in market forces and empowerment. Provide guardrails for what a good experience is.

episode_title: How marketplaces win: Liquidity, growth levers, quality, and more | Benjamin Lauzier (Lyft, Thumbtack, Reforge)
insight: Empowering your best users to onboard and coach new supply is a powerful, scalable, and cost-effective engine for growth, activation, and retention.
pull_quote: We would pay our best drivers $35 per mentor session... The benefits were absolutely mind-blowing and kind of unexpected... [it] allowed us to match most of Uber's footprint with a 10th or a 20th of the resources.

episode_title: Benjamin Mann
insight: In hyper-growth, exponential fields, top talent can create disproportionate value, making seemingly astronomical compensation a "cheap" investment.
pull_quote: A $100 million over a four-year package is actually pretty cheap compared to the value created for the business. We're in an unprecedented era of scale, and it's only going to get crazier.

episode_title: Benjamin Mann
insight: Don't just use new AI tools like old ones; push their limits and keep trying multiple times, even when they fail initially, to unlock true value.
pull_quote: People who use the new tools as if they were old tools tend to not succeed. If it doesn't work the first time, asking three more times because our success rate when you just completely start over and try again is much, much higher.

episode_title: Benjamin Mann
insight: Don't view safety and cutting-edge development as a trade-off; deep alignment research directly cultivates desirable product characteristics.
pull_quote: We've realized that it's actually kind of convex, working on one helps us with the other. Claude's character and personality, which people really loved, was directly a result of our alignment research.

episode_title: Benjamin Mann
insight: Being transparent about your AI's potential harms, even if it looks bad, earns vital trust from policymakers and differentiates your company.
pull_quote: There's a traditional mindset where it makes us look bad, but if you talk to policymakers, they really appreciate this kind of thing because they feel like we're giving them the straight talk and that's what we strive to do.

episode_title: Benjamin Mann
insight: In AI, build for where the puck is going in 6-12 months, not today, because what's 20% effective now will soon be 100%.
pull_quote: We think about skating to where the puck is going... really internalizing that of, 'Okay, don't build for today, build for six months from now, build for a year from now.' Things that aren't quite working will start working 100% of the time.

episode_title: Unpacking Amazon’s unique ways of working | Bill Carr (author of Working Backwards)
insight: Forget constraints and revenue goals; true product innovation starts by deeply understanding the customer's *unconstrained* needs and then building backward from there.
pull_quote: We took it as an article of faith: if we served customers well, things like sales, revenue, and share price would follow. When making a decision, start with what's best for the customer, then come backward from there.

episode_title: Unpacking Amazon’s unique ways of working | Bill Carr (author of Working Backwards)
